reasoning: C and D both separate the goal from the proxy metric (foot traffic) and flag the substituted-question trap, then propose value-per-visit and reach-of-non-users as success criteria. C edges ahead with the tightest calibration, ranked priors held loosely, a black-hat finding on day-shelter drift risk, a Friday-deployable test per direction, and a forced-collision method yielding non-obvious frames. D matches C on rigor and adds a two-stock model plus a premortem, but its framing is slightly more decorative than load-bearing. B is strong around three institutional monopolies with a clear first bet but interrogates the metric less. A is practical and actionable but asserts framings rather than stress-testing them and never separates proxy from goal.
